name: endive lentil salad
instructions: Sauté leeks and garlic in olive oil until softened. Add cooked, cold lentils on top and sauté briefly to drive off excess liquid — this is the key step that prevents the dish from going slimy later. Stir in the cream vinaigrette during this sauté so it coats the lentils and warms briefly. Remove from heat, fold in raw cut endives. Finish with slivered almonds for crunch.
notes:
Endives stay raw. Leeks (and garlic) are the only cooked components.

WHY SAUTE THE LENTILS: cold lentils tossed straight with a cream vinaigrette tend to seep liquid as the salad sits, and the dish goes slimy. A short sauté with the leeks (and folding the vinaigrette in while the pan is still warm) drives off the lentil moisture and lets the cream-and-acid coat the lentils properly.

NO TURNIPS — TOO MUCH BITTERNESS: I tried turnips here and they don't work. Endives are already bitter; turnips bring their own bitterness on top. The cream vinaigrette can balance one bitter element, but not two — with both, the dish reads muddled and aggressively bitter rather than clean. Pick one bitter green; here it's the endives.

SLIVERED ALMONDS FOR CRUNCH: replaces the turnip slot. Almonds add textural contrast without bitterness, and the toasted nut character works with the leek-and-cream base. Slivered (not whole or chopped) is the right form — they integrate with the lentils texturally instead of feeling like a bolt-on.

VINAIGRETTE ACID PREFERENCE: I prefer the lemon-juice version of the cream vinaigrette here (see cream-vinaigrette recipe — lemon juice and white wine vinegar are both valid acids). Lemon brightens the lentils and endives in a way vinegar doesn't quite match.

TARRAGON IS OPTIONAL: tarragon is the natural french herb to reach for in a leek-and-cream dish, and it works here. But the endives and leeks together already carry enough green/vegetal character that another herb isn't needed. Keep it as a secondary addition for when you want the extra herbal voice — don't feel obligated.
